entry.title: Re: Running KILL
entry.content:
@bo2l,

Thx for the reply.

 

Yes, I tried to run "SELECT @@spid" and it returned the one process that was running from "sp_who".

 

I also tried to run the second query but it didn't return any records.

 

So, what does it mean?

This is what happened:

1. I ran my C++ code with gdb and hit a problem inside transaction.

2. I interrupted that session without COMMIT/ROLLBACK, fixed the issue and restarted the gdb session.

3. At this point the program got stuck in side BEGIN TRANSACTION.

 

After googling, I saw that the way forward is "sp_who/KILL".

 

Do you know what can I do to identify the cause of my second gdb session to be stuck?

 

Thank you.

  • Integration using OPC-UA
  • Integration using AVEVA PI

Happy to help further if you can share more about your setup.

entry.content:
Items

CodeJam report live from Mexico with Tom and Rich

Building an LLM Agent using CAP

Sapphire 2026 highlights for SAP Business Data Cloud

- “Mastering SAP Data Architecture” new course https://learning.sap.com/courses/mastering-sap-data-architecture

Developer challenges May into June
- Challenge overview (May) https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/may-2026-developer-challenge-from-events-to-intelligence-building-an-event/ba-p/14386772
- Challenge overview (June) https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-cap-blog-posts/june-developer-challenge-cap-knowledge/ba-p/14405157 

Did You Know? #18 – Sharing and synchronising profiles
- ABAP Cleaner https://github.com/SAP/abap-cleaner

Chapter titles

00:00 Intro
00:07 CodeJam report live from Mexico with Tom and Rich
02:00 Building an LLM Agent using CAP
03:20 Sapphire 2026 highlights for SAP Business Data Cloud
05:21 Developer Challenges May into June
07:00 DYK #18 Sharing and synchronising profiles

Transcript

[Tom & Rich]
Hey, everybody. We're coming to you from a Code Jam Roadshow in Mexico. We started the week in Mexico City, Mexico. Rich, you had the first day. What were you teaching? Yeah, so we did ABAP Cloud and Joule, and we had a great day. We went through the exercises and saw how Joule can assist us in creating RAP applications quickly and easily. and everybody seemed to have a good time yeah and then I led day two which was the code-based agents code jam where we were looking at creating agents from scratch also how agents help us with coding activities we gave a little look at the new Joule Studio but also solved a crime who stole our painting from the gallery using multiple agents working together. So we get to see how different personas can be applied to agents, how different levels of knowledge and expertise can be applied to agents and how then they work together in a crew to solve a problem. Fun stuff. Yeah. And here we are Wednesday. It was a travel day to Monterey. I think that's where we are. That's all right. You see the beautiful mountains behind us. outside the city. And the moon, right? Yeah, and a crane, evidently. But now we're going to do it again. So all over again tomorrow, we're doing another code jam here in Monterey. Is it at the SAP offices? Tomorrow's at the SAP offices. Again, we're doing all ABAP cloud and Joule. Some of our friends are coming from Mexico City as well. And yeah, so we'll have a good time again. All right. Yeah. So until next time, maybe we'll be coming to a city near you in the near future check out all the new stuff that we have yep bye for now.

[Ajay]
Hello everyone agentic systems have been gaining significant traction lately and justifiably so they enable the development of ai systems capable of iteratively reasoning and executing toward the goal-oriented solutions while numerous frameworks and libraries exist for building standalone agents The SAP Cloud Application Programming Model now presents a distinct opportunity to architect agents that encapsulate domain -specific knowledge and seamlessly integrate with SAP ecosystem, including its native services and backend systems. We have a tutorial blog post by Johannes Vogt from CAP Enablement Team, where he walks us through the implementation of a lightweight LLM -powered agent leveraging the CAP framework. CAP integrates nicely with identity workflow and allows us to easily expose our services as tools for the agent, while also ensuring that all the necessary service level logic and permissions are applied. Since production-like qualities like authentication, authorization, and database transactions are handled by CAP, we can focus on the domain. There are further links to the blog post that you can explore for advanced topic and use cases. So give it a try today and begin your agentic journey using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model. Links to the blog post will be in the description below.

[Witalij]
If you are interested in all things data but missed Sapphire, or you just want the short version without sitting through hours and hours of sessions, our product expert at SAP, KP Sauer, has you covered. He recorded a few-minute recap of top new features in SAP Business Data Cloud to help you quickly catch up on the most important announcements. Here are some of the key topics he covered in his video. The announcement of the SAP Business AI Platform and what actually changed from an architectural perspective. Why SAP HANA Cloud is becoming a core component of the Business Data Cloud strategy. How Reltio and SAP Master Data Governance fit together inside BDC. And finally, Joule agents operating directly within the business data fabric. So, if you want the high -level takeaways without the noise, this is definitely You'll find the link in the description. Plus, this week, the Mastering SAP Data Architecture course officially went live on learning.sap.com, alongside the launch of the new SAP Certified Data Architect Certification. This is not a generic introduction
course. It is designed for data practitioners and data architects who need to operate on architectural depth and understand how enterprise data strategy is evolving in the AI era. The course covers topics like architecting data strategy for the agentic AI era, applying established frameworks including TOGAF, DAMA DMBOK and the SAP enterprise architecture framework in real-world scenarios and exploring concepts like data mesh, semantic layers, and knowledge graphs for enterprise AI. So if you are working in the enterprise data architecture, analytics, governance, or AI strategy, this is definitely worth your time as well.

[DJ]
The developer challenge for the month of May is coming to an end this week. Antonio did a great job accompanying and guiding us on a journey to learn about event driven architectures and AI. In week one, we acquainted ourselves with events. In week two, we connected to the broker and started to consume those events. In week three, we looked at vectorizing the event payload. And in this final week, week four, we're looking at storing vectors and enabling retrieval augmented generation. That's RAG. While this month's challenge is still active, June is just around the corner. So we wanted to give you a heads up that the next challenge is ready and will start on Monday. June's challenge encourages you to dig into the wonders and delights of the CAP documentation, aka CAPIRE, to unearth truth and beauty in this exemplary and valuable resource. Why? Because today, more than ever, we as developers need to ensure we retain knowledge and double down on our understanding of architectures, toolkits, frameworks, and perhaps most of all, of best practices. More on that in the challenge intro post, which is published now. When the challenge starts, you can also head back to this challenge intro post to find your way to the tasks. Good luck, have fun, and as always, happy learning.

[Sheena]
Did you know that you can maintain a synchronized ABAP profile across your team by creating and sharing a team profile in ABAP Cleaner? Usually, ABAP Cleaner profiles are created locally based on the required rules. Let us consider a scenario where a project team would like to come up with a synchronized profile that can be used by everyone in the team. Here, the lead developer or architect can formulate a profile where all the required rules are activated and this profile is then exported using the export button to a synchronized folder, which may be the team's OneDrive or SharePoint. Team members can then use this profile by clicking on the folders button to add the synchronized folder as an additional read-only source of team profiles. In case of any further changes, we can simply export it again to override the synchronized folder and thereby sharing the updates to everyone, provided it's not the default profile.

entry.title: Re: Not able to add a new Routine Number inside the Prici...
entry.content:
Good day @Ramkinker 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshot and the requirement, the key point is that the old on-premise approach of copying a standard VOFM/R program into a Z routine is not available in the cloud system. In the Public Cloud, pricing routine extensions are only possible through the released pricing BAdIs in the Custom Fields app, and only for the supported pricing use cases such as condition amount, condition base value and pricing requirements. See KBA 2885513 and 2756560 for more insights.

In your case, the BAdIs you already checked are the correct released ones, but they do not provide a changing parameter to replace or overwrite the material group used by the standard routine. This means you cannot create a new routine number in the same way you did in on-premise and use it to change the default material group logic inside the standard pricing routine.

So, in simple terms the following apply:

  • You cannot copy a standard pricing routine into a Z routine like in on premise.
  • You cannot directly change the standard material group determination used by SAP standard pricing routine logic.
  • The pricing BAdIs listed can influence condition amount, condition base value, or whether a condition should be considered but they do not allow you to change the standard material group field used internally by the pricing routine.
  • If your requirement is only to include or exclude a condition based on another material group related value, then the Public Cloud offers you the possible approach of using pricing requirement BAdI.
  • If the requirement is to determine prices or discounts using another material grouping field, the better design is to model this through pricing condition tables, access sequences, custom fields for pricing, or condition records instead of trying to change the standard routine.

There is also extensibility possibility for pricing condition records using custom fields. This means, if the field you need is not the standard material group but a different product attribute, you can evaluate whether it can be added as a custom field for pricing and then used in condition records.

Basically, this means you have two realistic options:

Option 1 (cleanest approach) is for you to use pricing configuration instead of a new routine. If the requirement is to price based on another grouping logic, create or adjust the pricing configuration using:

  • Condition table
  • Access sequence
  • Condition type
  • Pricing procedure
  • Condition records

Option 2 if for you to use a pricing requirement BAdI only to control whether the condition is active. If the requirement is not to change the material group itself but only to decide whether the pricing condition should apply or not, then use:

  • PRICING_DOCUMENT_REQUIREMENT
  • PRCG_DOCUMENT_ITEM_REQUIREMENT

These can be used to return whether a pricing condition should be considered. However, they should not be expected to change the standard material group or the standard access field used for pricing. Note that the logic is used to determine whether a condition is considered during pricing.

For your exact requirement, if you need a new routine number that changes the standard routine’s material group logic, then this is not supported in the cloud system with the released extensibility options.

The best direction is therefore:

  1. Check whether the “other Material Group” can be represented as a standard pricing field, product hierarchy, product attribute or a custom field enabled for pricing.
  2. If yes, create a custom condition table and access sequence using that field.
  3. Assign the access sequence to the relevant condition type.
  4. Maintain the condition records in Manage Price-Sales.
  5. Test the pricing result in a sales order
  6. If the field cannot be made available for pricing and cannot be handled through the released BAdIs, you may consider raising an influence request.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Embracing the Future at Sapphire 2026 - A Journey with Migration and Modernization Assistants
entry.content:
Welcome to Sapphire 2026, where the future meets enterprise transformation! This year, attendees had the privilege of diving into the world of Migration and Modernization Assistants, a vision brought to life by industry leaders Soeren Ruder and Sonja Liénard during a riveting session at the conference.

Reflecting on the Journey

The session kicked off with Soeren, CPO at Cloud ALM, drawing us into the transformational journey that began back in 2021 with the launch of RISE with SAP. Although initially branded as "Business Transformation as a Service," Soeren was candid about the lack of substantial transformation at the onset. However, fast forward to today, and we see a company that has made significant strides with impressive acquisitions like Signavio, LeanIX, and WalkMe, reiterating their commitment to authentic business transformation.

Recognizing that transformation is not without its complexities and costs, Soeren emphasized the role of the latest AI capabilities and emerging technologies in addressing these challenges, marking 2026 as a significant milestone in embedding migration and modernization into the core of business transformation motions.

Introducing the Magnificent Seven: Migration and Modernization Assistants

Soeren's presentation took us through the innovative toolchain designed to minimize risks and costs associated with migration projects. Here’s a glimpse into these groundbreaking assistants:

  1. System Analysis Assistant: The cornerstone of transformation projects, this tool provides insights into system data, custom code, and overall requirements, laying the groundwork for seamless migration.
  2. Data Management Assistant: Tackling data inconsistencies and offering actionable solutions to expedite the migration process, this assistant leverages AI for rapid and effective data cleanup.
  3. Custom Code Assistant: With Joule for Developer, this assistant not only ensures code compatibility with SAP S/4HANA but also dives deep into semantic understanding, offering a path from the complexity of custom code to optimized standard processes.
  4. Configuration Assistant: Automating system configuration by smartly combining best practice models with user-defined scopes, this assistant sets up systems efficiently.
  5. Test Management Assistant: Redefining test case creation with AI, this assistant collaborates with Tricentis to reduce manual effort, accelerating testing phases with precision and automation.
  6. Rollout Assistant: Simplifying regional expansions, this tool enables parallel rollouts by ensuring template compatibility and adaptations.
  7. Project Management Assistant: Moving beyond traditional project oversight methods, this tool makes project management dynamic and responsive, integrating intelligent feedback with a single source of truth for progress tracking.

Live Demonstration: A Real-Time Experience

The session transitioned to an enthralling demo led by Sonja, where attendees witnessed the synergy of these assistants within a unified platform. The demo illustrated processes across three different roles - project manager, custom code lead and developer - demonstrating how these assistants streamline tasks and optimize project execution.

Through strategic task delegation and AI-powered automated code remediation, the demo revealed a future where ERP migration is transformed from labor-intensive endeavors to efficient, outcome-driven processes.

Commitment to Excellence: SAP’s Vision Realized

The announcement of Siemens as an early adopter resonates with SAP’s confidence in these Migration and Modernization Assistants. Siemens’ alignment with SAP's goals of reducing migration efforts is a testament to the transformative power these tools hold in real-world applications.

Explore More at Sapphire 2026

With numerous sessions exploring the depths of data management, custom code optimization, and task automation, Sapphire 2026 promises a wealth of knowledge and innovation. Attendees are encouraged to visit the Agent Led Tool Chain booth for deeper insights and discussions with experts.

As we conclude this session, it's evident that the Migration and Modernization Assistants are not merely tools—they are harbingers of a new era in enterprise transformation. Enjoy the Sapphire experience, and here's to shaping the future of digital business transformation!

Thank you for joining us at Sapphire 2026.

entry.title: Re: find sql anywhere development files
entry.content:
The point is that you seem to be using some sort of make tool. The environment variable is SQLANY17 (don't know where you got the braces from). Many years ago, the make tool we used would have expected $(SQLANY17). I am suggesting to you that you use the internet to find out how to reference an environment variable using whatever make tool you employ. Here is a piece of an ancient makefile that we used 30 years ago.

!ifndef H_DIR                                          
!   define H_DIR $(DBDIR)\h                            
!endif                                                 
!ifndef WSQLH_DIR                                      
!   define WSQLH_DIR $(H_DIR)\wsqlh                    
!endif                                                 
!ifndef NEWDBDIR                                       
!   define NEWDBDIR $(DBDIR)\newstore                  
!endif                                                 
!ifndef HVER_DIR_ROOT                                  
!   define HVER_DIR_ROOT    h\h$(WSQLVER)              
!endif                                                 
!define HVER_DIR            $(DBDIR)\$(HVER_DIR_ROOT)

entry.title: Ledger not active in Chart of Depreciation & FYV mismatch (Copied Chart of Dep. on Practice Server)
entry.content:
hello SAP Experts,

I am currently setting up a configuration on a practice server and am hitting a roadblock while trying to assign/register a new Company Code (GM01) to a Chart of Depreciation.

For context, I copied the Chart of Depreciation (GM01) from another country's standard template (such as US / India) into my current setup. When saving, I am receiving the following two hard errors:

  1. "Fiscal year variants in company code GM01 have differing start dates"

  2. "Ledger Q1 defined in CoCd GM01 is not active in chart of depreciation GM01"

What I have tried so far:

  • I tried creating a new ledger and activating it with the Company Code again, but the system continues to state that the specific ledger is not active.

Since this is a practice environment and the Chart of Depreciation was copied across different country standards, I suspect there is a fundamental mismatch in the underlying calendar/fiscal settings and ledger mappings.

Could anyone please advise on how to resolve this? Specifically:

  1. How can I align the differing start dates for the Fiscal Year Variants caused by this copy?

  2. What are the exact configuration steps to successfully activate/map Ledger Q1 within this copied Chart of Depreciation GM01?

Any step-by-step guidance or advice on how to clean up these copied settings would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your help!

Best regards,

entry.title: Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products
entry.content:
Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products

Introduction

In high-volume warehouse environments, it is common to receive multiple outbound deliveries containing identical products for different customers.
Executing picking separately for each outbound delivery often results in:

  • excessive warehouse task creation,

  • repeated travel paths,

  • unnecessary picker movement,

  • increased labor effort,

  • and reduced warehouse efficiency.

SAP EWM provides the capability to optimize this process using Combined Picking, where products from multiple outbound deliveries are consolidated into a single warehouse order for execution.

This article demonstrates a practical warehouse execution scenario involving:

  • 3 outbound deliveries,

  • common products across all deliveries,

  • single combined warehouse order creation,

  • consolidated warehouse task execution,

  • and final distribution back to respective deliveries.

Business Scenario

A warehouse receives 3 outbound deliveries for different customers:

Customer Outbound Delivery

Walmart SuperCenter80010771
Costco Wholesale80010772
Albertsons80010773

All 3 deliveries contain the same products:

  • Product 1

  • Product 2

  • Product 3

Instead of generating separate picking tasks for each delivery, SAP EWM combines the picking quantities into a single warehouse execution process.

Delivery Overview

Delivery 1 – Walmart SuperCenter

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 11 EA
250055Product 22 EA
250056Product 33 EA

Delivery 2 – Costco Wholesale

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 14 EA
250055Product 25 EA
250056Product 36 EA

Delivery 3 – Albertsons

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 17 EA
250055Product 28 EA
250056Product 39 EA

Combined Warehouse Order Creation

SAP EWM combines all 9 delivery items into a single warehouse order in queue:

EACHESPICK

Instead of:

  • 9 individual warehouse tasks,

  • multiple travel paths,

  • and repetitive picking,

the system intelligently consolidates quantities for common products.

This significantly improves:

  • picker productivity,

  • travel optimization,

  • warehouse throughput,

  • and labor efficiency.

Combined Warehouse Tasks

The warehouse picker now executes combined picking quantities:

Material Description Total Qty to Pick UoM

250054Product 112EA
250055Product 215EA
250056Product 318EA

Total:

45 EA across 3 products

The picker performs picking in a single wave instead of executing repetitive delivery-based picks.

Picking Execution

During execution:

  • picker retrieves consolidated quantities,

  • products are staged temporarily,

  • quantities are later sorted/distributed back to respective outbound deliveries,

  • and final loading is executed customer-wise.

This approach minimizes:

  • repeated aisle movement,

  • RF scanning effort,

  • and warehouse congestion.

Distribution Back to Deliveries

After combined picking execution, quantities are redistributed back to the original deliveries.

Material Walmart Costco Albertsons Total Picked

Product 11 EA4 EA7 EA12 EA
Product 22 EA5 EA8 EA15 EA
Product 33 EA6 EA9 EA18 EA

This ensures:

  • customer-level delivery integrity,

  • accurate outbound processing,

  • and optimized warehouse execution simultaneously.

Operational Benefits

Reduced Warehouse Travel Time

Picker visits storage bins once for combined quantities instead of repeating trips per delivery.

Improved Warehouse Productivity

Fewer warehouse tasks and consolidated execution improve throughput.

Lower RF Interaction Effort

Reduced scanning and confirmation steps for warehouse operators.

Better Wave Optimization

Combined picking aligns well with wave management and outbound staging strategies.

Reduced Queue Volume

Warehouse order consolidation minimizes queue and task fragmentation.

Important Design Considerations

Before implementing combined picking, warehouses should evaluate:

  • customer segregation requirements,

  • staging space availability,

  • sorting effort after picking,

  • handling unit strategies,

  • transportation/loading constraints,

  • and warehouse process type alignment.

Combined picking is especially effective for:

  • retail distribution,

  • wholesale fulfillment,

  • FTL outbound scenarios,

  • and high-volume repetitive product movements.

Key Takeaways

This scenario demonstrates how SAP EWM can intelligently optimize outbound execution by consolidating common products across multiple deliveries into a single warehouse execution flow.

Process Summary:

  • 3 deliveries

  • 3 customers

  • Common products across all deliveries

  • 1 combined warehouse order

  • 45 EA picked in a consolidated wave

  • Quantities redistributed back to original deliveries

The result is:

higher warehouse efficiency, reduced picker movement, optimized labor utilization, and improved outbound execution performance.

Conclusion

Combined Picking in SAP EWM is not merely a technical warehouse task consolidation feature — it is a warehouse execution optimization strategy.

When designed correctly, it can significantly improve:

  • warehouse throughput,

  • labor productivity,

  • picking efficiency,

  • and operational scalability in high-volume distribution environments.

As warehouses continue moving toward larger fulfillment volumes and tighter outbound timelines, combined picking becomes an increasingly valuable execution strategy within SAP EWM.

entry.title: SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA のためのSAPノート適用について
entry.content:
お世話になります。

SAPノート3059197 - SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades に従ってアセスメントを実施するにあたり、
SAPノート 3365856 - Note Analyzer Input File for SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades. により、ノートアナライザを実行しました。
その結果、
 Preparation SAP Note Check Phase 及び SAP Note Check Phase にて、109件のSAPノートが適用候補としてリストアップされました。
この２つの SAP Note Check PhaseでリストアップされたSAPノートの全ては、SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgradesのための修正（先行開発）であり、業務のロジックや業務画面の構成等に影響を与えるものは無いと考えてよいでしょうか？

ひとまず、BASISチームで109件のSAPノートを確認したうえで、業務チームに照会する予定ですが、上記の考え方が正しければ、業務チームへの照会を省略することが出来るのではないかと想定しております。

お手数ですが、ご教示ください。

SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgrades の分析結果の全レポートについて、サンプル版がありましたら提供していただけないでしょうか？

現在、5月末の予定で分析結果のレポートを確認する予定でしたが、適用対象のSAPノートの数が多く、業務影響の有無の調査に時間がかかっております。

もし、レポートのサンプル版が入手できれば、SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA upgradesの分析結果としてどのような情報が得られるのかを事前に確認できるので大変助かります。

When use GAP and when WRICEF ?

NOTE TO SAP SUPPORT: I give my approval for SAP Support to reproduce the described issue, while connected to my impacted non-production environments, using any steps provided in the description below or provided later, even if the steps result in a change being made, until the issue is closed.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Alm classification / sub-classification doc...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Please refer to below SAP Community article that provides further explanation regarding the different types in SAP Cloud ALM:

Task and Requirement Management – SAP Cloud ALM or... - SAP Community 

  • Gap: A completely new development which needs to be specified in detail with significant ‘as-is /to-be’ evaluation, often with no technical information or idea how to realize it at the beginning. The technical design is fully done by the developer with the help of a technical design document.  
  • WRICEF (workflows, reports, interfaces, conversions, enhancements, and forms): Typical extensions, where no business background needs to be described. The consultants often know already how to implement and configure. Specifications are often already a mixture of functional and technical design. 
  • Fit (configuration): For changes without coding adjustments, but only customizing. The specification is often an existing standard configuration guide and only customizing documentation needs to be maintained 
  • Non-Functional requirements are used for documentation upload or parameter settings without the need to document a functional specification.  

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Managing Batches in SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
When working with products that require batch tracking like food items, pharmaceuticals, or materials with expiration dates having a robust batch management system is essential. SAP Logistics Management offers integrated batch handling that connects seamlessly with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, ensuring traceability throughout your warehouse operations.

When working with products that require batch tracking like food items, pharmaceuticals, or materials with expiration dates having a robust batch management system is essential. SAP Logistics Management offers integrated batch handling that connects seamlessly with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, ensuring traceability throughout your warehouse operations.

Batches in SAP Logistics Management

Batches are centrally managed master data that capture critical product information. This data is replicated from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to SAP Logistics Management using the Schedule Data Replication app in SAP Logistics Management, making it available across all warehouse processes. Pre-requisite for the replication of batches from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to SAP Logistics Management is, that the batch level is defined on material level in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition customizing.

The following batch characteristics are stored in SAP Logistics Management and can be found in the Display Batches app:

  • Shelf life expiration date
  • Manufacturing date
  • Batch status
  • Country or region of origin
  • Supplier and supplier batch number
  • Whether the batch is marked for deletion

 

How Batches Flow Through Your Warehouse

Inbound Operations

A batch is entered in the inbound delivery item in SAP S/4HANA and the inbound delivery is distributed automatically to SAP Logistics Management. You can view the batch for the item in the Monitor Logistical Demands and Manage Inbound Warehouse Activities apps.

Note: Batch split is not supported.

 

Stock Management

Batches serve as quant-differentiating attributes, meaning your system tracks inventory at the batch level. This granular visibility is available through the Manage Stock app.

 

The Upload Stock app supports now batch-managed products. You can include the batch directly in the template before uploading.

 

Warehouse Tasks

During picking and putaway, batch information is visible in both the Process Warehouse Tasks app and the SAP Warehouse Logistics mobile app (version 1.4), ensuring warehouse operatives always know which batch they're handling.

 

Outbound Operations

A batch is entered in the outbound delivery item in SAP S/4HANA and the outbound delivery is distributed to SAP Logistics Management. The batch follows through picking, packing, staging, and goods issue, maintaining complete traceability until the product leaves your facility. 

Notes:

  • Batch split is not supported
  • The batch is not displayed in transportation relevant documents in SAP Logistics Management

 

Conclusion

Managing batches doesn't have to be complicated. With the right system in place, you gain the visibility and control needed to meet regulatory requirements while keeping operations running smoothly. The above mentioned is just the beginning of the batch journey in SAP Logistics Management so stay tuned.

entry.title: Re: Email Processing & SOST Behavior in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, email outputs (like payment advices) are not managed through the classic SOST/SCOT transactions as in on-premise systems. Instead, they rely on Output Parameter Determination (OPD), Business Partner email data, and the SAP Output Management framework. If emails are not being sent, the issue is usually due to missing recipient configuration in OPD or restrictions on sender/recipient domains.

Key Steps to Test & Troubleshoot Email Outputs

1. Verify Recipient Setup

Output Parameter Determination (OPD)

Go to the OPD app and check the determination step “Email Recipient” for the relevant document type (e.g., Payment Advice). Ensure a valid recipient email is explicitly defined. This overrides Business Partner data.

Business Partner Master Data

Confirm the email is correctly maintained under the Address tab or Contact Person relationship. Ensure the Valid To date for the contact person is set to 12/31/9999; expired contacts will not be read.

2. Check Allowed Domains
SAP Cloud restricts sender/recipient domains for security. Errors like “Sender domain not allowed” or “Recipient address not allowed” indicate domain whitelisting issues. Work with your Basis/Cloud administrator to ensure your company’s domain is registered in the Communication Arrangement for SMTP.

3. Monitor Email Status
Email Monitoring Apps (e.g., Output Management → Monitor Output) show only limited status. Unlike on-premise SOST, you won’t see full SMTP traces. If the status is “Error,” check the Application Log (SLG1) for SAPconnect-related messages.

4. Background Jobs
Ensure the RSCONN01 job (responsible for processing email queues) is scheduled. Without this, emails remain in “Queued” status.

Having said that, the action plan for you is

  • Re-check OPD rules for Payment Advice → ensure “Email Recipient” is defined.
  • Validate Business Partner email and contact validity.
  • Confirm your sender domain is allowed in Communication Arrangement.
  • Ask Basis team to verify SMTP configuration and background jobs.
  • Use Application Log (SLG1) for deeper error details beyond the monitoring app.

entry.title: Customer-specific Lot Sizing Procedure in ePP/DS
entry.content:
Dear PP/DS Gurus,

I have a questions if I could configure a customer-specific lot sizing procedure and apply it to standard Heuristics, e.g. SAP_PP_002.

Due to manucacturing constraints, my client wants to create planned orders using following lot sizing rules.
- Fixed Lot Size : 30, Max Lot Size : 40

For a new demand, planned orders are basically created based on "Fixed Lot Size". When if uncovered demand quantities remain, the order should be created as follows.
- Case 1 : When uncovered demand quantities > "Max Lot Size" - "Fixed Lot Size", a new planned order should be created by uncovered demand quantities
- Case 2 : When uncovered demand quantities < "Max Lot Size" - "Fixed Lot Size", the uncovered demand quantities should be merged with the last planned order

Here are examaples.
- Case 1 : Demands quantities - 92
                  -> 3 planned orders are created first based on "Fixed Lot Size"
                  -> Planned Order #1(30), Planned Order #2(30), Planned Order #3(30)
                  -> Uncovered demand quantities(2) < "Max Lot Size" - "Fixed Lot Size"(10)
                  -> The final results would be  Planned Order #1(30), Planned Order #2(30), Planned Order #3(32)
- Case 2 : DEmands quantities - 110
                  -> 3 planned orders are created first based on "Fixed Lot Size"
                  -> Planned Order #1(30), Planned Order #2(30), Planned Order #3(30)
                  -> Uncovered demand quantities(20) < "Max Lot Size" - "Fixed Lot Size"(10)
                  -> The final results would be  Planned Order #1(30), Planned Order #2(30), Planned Order #3(30), Planned Order #4(20)

I'm trying to figure out how to configure these requirements using "Lot Sizing Profile", "Rouding Profile - Static, Dynamic, Quantity addition/Subtraction" but I could not find any clues.

So I just want to know if there are any User-Exits or Enhancements to reslove these requiments.

 

Thanks in advance for your cooperation. 

Best Regards,
Sean Lee

entry.title: Re: Output Parameters for Handling Unit
entry.content:
Explore the below options

1) Custom Fields and Logic app
You can add a custom HU field (e.g., “Finalized Indicator”) using Custom Fields and Logic. This field can be exposed in Output Parameter Determination (OPD) as a condition parameter.

2) Released BAdIs (if available)
In Public Cloud, you can only implement SAP‑released BAdIs listed in the Extensibility Catalog. If there is a released BAdI for Handling Unit events (e.g., “HU placed in GI bin”), you can use it to set the custom field. If no such BAdI exists, you cannot inject custom ABAP logic — instead, you must rely on the standard OPD parameters (HU Step Completed, Storage Bin, Storage Process).

3) Fallback if no BAdI exists
Configure OPD rules directly and trigger HU_SHIPPING_LABEL only when HU Step Completed = true and Storage Bin = GI bin. This avoids multiple prints during intermediate HU changes, without needing a custom field.

entry.title: Re: unloading location timing is different
entry.content:
Why the mismatch happens is because of the following:

Delivery Note (DN)
Dates/times are stored in the ERP system (S/4HANA) in local time zone or user time zone.

Freight Order (FO)
When the DN is transferred to TM, the times are converted into the planning time zone defined in TM customizing.

If your ERP system and TM system (or the FO location master data) are set to different time zones, you will see exactly this kind of one‑hour offset.

So the recommended approach is

  • Verify the time zone maintained in the unloading location master data in TM. Align it with the DN’s plant/location time zone.
  • Check the planning time zone in TM customizing. If it differs from the business requirement, adjust it.
  • If you need FO times to always match DN times, ensure both systems (ERP and TM) use the same time zone for the relevant locations.

entry.title: Re: Two Way Match Supplier Criteria
entry.content:
Below are the recommended approaches for transition

Segment suppliers
Apply two‑way only to service vendors, recurring contracts, and low‑risk categories. Keep three‑way for high‑value, physical goods, or new suppliers.

Set tolerance thresholds
Define acceptable variances in PO vs invoice matching to reduce exceptions.

Automate controls
Use AP automation tools to enforce rules consistently and flag anomalies.

Monitor performance
Track disputes, overpayments, and fraud attempts after transition. Adjust supplier eligibility accordingly.

Communicate with suppliers
Ensure they understand the streamlined process and compliance expectations.

If you want to build a sustainable solution I would recommend you to look at Business Data Cloud (BDC) and use Datasphere (DSP), (SAP) Databricks and SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) to manage your data in DSP, share data products with Databricks via Zero Copy Delta Sharing (and vice versa). You can then consume data via a live connection from SAC to DSP.

entry.title: Re: System Requirements SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content: Hi Marco, I think, there is no definitive answer to this question. The requirements depend on the browser, network, operating system, other system loads, and the complexity of usage. As a rough guideline for basic access as a viewer, a modern computer with the latest version of Chrome or Edge, about 4 GB of RAM, and a standard dual-core processor should suffice. For a comfortable user experience, however, 8 GB of RAM or more, a current quad-core processor, and a stable, fast internet connection are recommended. Hard drive space is not an issue for the SAC. More important are a current operating system, sufficient browser cache, and a monitor with appropriate resolution, especially for complex stories.

entry.title: Re: Multiple Cost Objects in SES Despite No Cost Center i...
entry.content:
Why you are seeing multiple cost objects is because of the following:

Automatic account assignment derivation
Even if no cost center is in the upload file, the system can derive account assignments from:

  • The purchase order item (e.g., WBS element, internal order, network).
  • Default account assignment rules in configuration (e.g., valuation class, account determination).
  • Business rules in CPMM (Commercial Project Management) that link services to project objects.

Invoice integration
When the SES is transferred to invoice, the system re‑derives account assignments based on PO + SES + project settings. That is why you are seeing an additional cost object appear at invoice stage.

Check the following:

1) Purchase Order account assignment category

Verify whether the PO item is linked to a project (WBS, network) or another object. SES inherits this automatically.

2) Default account assignment settings
In Public Cloud, SSCUIs and configuration for account determination may insert defaults even if the upload file is blank.

3) Mass upload template mapping
Ensure the template fields are aligned with the intended account assignment category. Sometimes blank fields trigger fallback logic.

4) CPMM integration
If CPMM is active, SES items may be linked to project objects automatically, even without a cost center.

In short, the system is not “inventing” cost objects, it is deriving them from PO/CPMM rules when SES is created and invoiced. The fix usually involves tightening PO account assignment categories or adjusting CPMM integration so only the intended object is carried forward.

entry.title: Re: Impact Analysis (Change Record) – “Key is invalid” er...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check whether the information helps you solve your issue:

Technical BOM Identifier Requirement

  • Question: Does Impact Analysis with CDS A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1 require a technical BOM identifier (BillOfMaterial UUID) instead of the functional key (Material + Plant + Usage + Alternative)?
  • Answer: The CDS view A_BILLOFMATERIAL_1 might require a technical identifier like the BillOfMaterial UUID for precise mapping and execution. This is because the functional key might not uniquely identify the BOM in all scenarios, especially when dealing with complex BOM structures.

2. Limitations on BOM Usage

  • Question: Is there any standard limitation on BOM Usage (e.g., Engineering BOM not supported)?
  • Answer: There are no explicit limitations mentioned for BOM Usage in the context of Impact Analysis. However, ensure that the BOM Usage is correctly configured and supported within your system’s scope. For instance, some BOM usages might require specific configurations or activations, as seen with Engineering/Design BOMs in certain contexts.

3. Additional Configuration for BOM Key Mapping

  • Question: Is additional configuration required to map the BOM key correctly between Change Record UI and CDS view?
  • Answer: Yes, additional configuration might be necessary. Ensure that the UI Field Mapping Customization is correctly set up  to align with the CDS view’s requirements. This includes ensuring that all necessary fields are mapped and that the data types and formats are consistent. For more information, see Impact Analysis - Sample Scenario.

4. Known Issues or OSS Notes

  • Question: Are there known issues or OSS notes related to this error in version 2602?
  • Answer: While specific OSS notes for version 2602 are not mentioned, it is advisable to check the SAP Support Portal for any updates or notes related to Impact Analysis and BOM configurations. Additionally, ensure that all relevant business catalogs and authorizations are correctly assigned to avoid authorization errors.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Customer-specific Lot Sizing Procedure in ePP/DS
entry.content:
In standard SAP S/4HANA PP/DS heuristics (like SAP_PP_002), you cannot configure such a nuanced “fixed lot size + max lot size with merging logic” purely through Lot Sizing Procedures or Rounding Profiles. The standard lot sizing methods (fixed lot size, rounding value, minimum/maximum lot size, etc.) cover simpler rules, but they do not support the conditional merging you described (e.g., “if remainder < max – fixed, merge into last order, otherwise create a new one”).

So what you can do is check BAdI /SAPAPO/LOT_SIZE which is the standard extension point for custom lot sizing in PP/DS. Develop a custom lot sizing procedure and code the logic you described (fixed lot size, max lot size, conditional merging). Assign your custom procedure in the product master or heuristic settings, so it applies during planning runs.

entry.title: Re: How to add wait in SAP C4C Using ABSL Code
entry.content:
Hi Sri,

You can try the below approach for implementing a wait condition in ABSL. This should work:

import AP.PlatinumEngineering;

SessionContext.Wait(5);

The SessionContext.Wait() method pauses the execution for the specified duration before proceeding to the next statement. Please test it in your scenario and let us know if you encounter any issues.

Thanks.

entry.title: Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Message Types in Practice
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Prerequisites
  • Message Types at a Glance
  • Plain Text Messages
  • Card Messages
  • List Messages
  • Carousel Messages
  • Media Messages
  • Buttons Messages
  • Likert Scale Messages
  • UI Integration Cards
  • Illustrated Messages
  • Common Pitfalls
  • UI Constraints and Limits
  • Try It Yourself
  • What is Next
  • Resources

Introduction

This post is part of the Joule Capability Development Blog Series. The first post covers setting up your development environment and building your first capability. This post builds on that foundation and dives into message types.

When building SAP Joule capabilities, the message: block in your function YAML controls how Joule renders the response. Choose the wrong type and a clean OData result becomes a wall of raw text. Choose the right one and the same data becomes a structured card, a list, or a rich UI5 Integration Card that feels like a native Fiori element.

Joule can also generate responses automatically using its LLM based on the data your function returns; see Gen AI Response Generation in the Joule Development Guide. The message: block gives you explicit control over the layout when the generated output is not structured enough for your use case.

The official Joule Development Guide documents the available message types, but it stops short of showing what each one looks like in practice or when to prefer one over another. This post fills that gap using the public Northwind OData service introduced in Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started. The examples here give a practical overview of each type and the decisions that go into choosing one over another.

Each message type has more fields and options than what fits in a single example, so treat this as a starting point rather than a complete reference. For the full SAP Help Documentation, see the Messages page of the Joule Development Guide. You can try the message types yourself using the digital assistants with their capabilities available in the respective folder of the GitHub Repo; see the Try It Yourself  section below.

One of the sample capabilities, Buttons, acts as a navigable launcher across all the others. Ask Joule to show different message types in action and you get one clickable postback button per message type. Clicking a button sends the right prompt back to Joule and triggers that capability without you having to type anything. You can read the sections in order or jump around in chat using the launcher.

Prerequisites

Complete Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started before starting here. You need the Joule CLI installed and a BTP destination named Northwind.

Message Types at a Glance

TypeWhat Joule rendersBest for
textPlain text, supports MarkdownFree-form answers, errors, simple facts
cardTitle, subtitle, image, status, descriptionSingle-record preview with optional drill-down
buttonsSummary text with clickable action buttonsGuided next-step selection
quickRepliesInline suggestion chips below a messageShort yes/no or option selection
carouselHorizontally scrollable set of cardsComparing a small set of records
listVertically scrollable list of itemsSearch results, multi-record display
mediaImage, video, or audio embedEmbedding a product demo, keynote, or how-to video
client_dataSilent payload for client-side logicTriggering navigation without visible output
ui5integrationCardFull UI5 Integration Card via manifest JSONRich Fiori-style grouped field display
illustrated_messageSAP illustration with title and descriptionSuccess/error/empty state feedback
likert_scaleClickable scale with labeled options (1–7)In-chat surveys and satisfaction ratings

This post covers text, card, buttons, list, carousel, media, ui5integrationCard, illustrated_message, and likert_scale. quickReplies is included in the table for completeness but not demonstrated separately, as it behaves like a lighter-weight buttons message.

Plain Text Messages

The text type is the simplest message type available. It renders the content string directly in the chat window and optionally interprets Markdown. Use it when you need a free-form answer, an error message, or a brief fact where visual structure would add no value.

# northwind_product_text/functions/get_product_text.yaml (relevant section)
- type: message
  message:
    type: text
    markdown: true
    content: |
      - **Product:** <? product.ProductName ?>
      - **Price:** $<? product.UnitPrice ?>
      - **In Stock:** <? product.UnitsInStock ?> units

Set markdown: true at the same level as content to render bold, italics, and lists. Add delay: <seconds> to introduce a pause before the message appears, useful when chaining multiple messages. For error states, a text message with a clear explanation is more useful to the user than an empty card.

Message Type: Text

Example use cases: single values, error messages, displaying markdown, or any response where structured layout would distract rather than help.

Card Messages

The card type renders a structured tile with a title, subtitle, icon or image, status badge, and a Markdown-enabled description block. It is the visual equivalent of a single-record detail page condensed into a chat message.

# northwind_product_card/functions/get_product_card.yaml (relevant section)
- type: message
  message:
    type: card
    content:
      title: <? product.ProductName ?>
      subtitle: "Unit Price: $<? product.UnitPrice ?>"
      imageUrl: "sap-icon://product"
      imageStyle: avatar
      description:
        value: "<? product.UnitsInStock ?> in stock · <? product.QuantityPerUnit ?>"
        markdown: false
      enableDetailView: false
      sections:
        - title: Stock Details
          attributes:
            - label: Units on Order
              value: <? product.UnitsOnOrder ?>
            - label: Reorder Level
              value: <? product.ReorderLevel ?>
            - label: Discontinued
              value: "<? product.Discontinued == true ? 'Yes' : 'No' ?>"

The sample above shows the core fields. Beyond those, the card type supports additional options: statusState accepts success, warning, error, and information, mapping to the standard SAP semantic colors (green, orange, red, blue). Set enableDetailView: true to make the card expandable. Add a buttons array under content to append action buttons directly below the card body, following the same structure described in the Buttons section.

Message Type: Card

When to use: any single-record result where the user benefits from seeing title, status, and a few key fields at a glance without leaving the chat.

List Messages

The list type renders a vertically scrollable container of items. Each item supports a title, subtitle, description, icon, and its own buttons. Use it for search results or any response that returns more than one record.

The list function accepts an optional category_id parameter. When provided it adds an OData $filter to the request, scoping results to a single category. When omitted it returns the first five products from the full catalog. This makes the same function reusable both as a standalone capability and as the target of a postback from the carousel described in the next section.

# northwind_product_list/functions/get_product_list.yaml
parameters:
  - name: category_id
    optional: true

action_groups:
  - condition: category_id != null
    actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Products?$filter=CategoryID%20eq%20<? category_id ?>&$select=ProductName,UnitPrice,UnitsInStock,QuantityPerUnit,UnitsOnOrder,ReorderLevel,Discontinued&$top=5"
        result_variable: api_result
  - condition: category_id == null
    actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Products?$select=ProductName,UnitPrice,UnitsInStock,QuantityPerUnit,UnitsOnOrder,ReorderLevel,Discontinued&$top=5"
        result_variable: api_result

The message block uses Handlebars to iterate the result array:

  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: products
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: message
        scripting_type: handlebars
        message: >
          {
            "type": "list",
            "content": {
              "title": "Northwind Product Catalog",
              "subtitle": "Showing first 5 products",
              "enableDetailView": true,
              "elements": [
                {{#eachJoin products}}
                {
                  "title": "{{ProductName}}",
                  "subtitle": "${{UnitPrice}}",
                  "description": "{{UnitsInStock}} units in stock",
                  "imageUrl": "sap-icon://product",
                  "imageStyle": "avatar",
                  "sections": [
                    {
                      "title": "Stock Information",
                      "attributes": [
                        { "label": "Quantity per Unit", "value": "{{QuantityPerUnit}}" },
                        { "label": "Units on Order", "value": "{{UnitsOnOrder}}" },
                        { "label": "Reorder Level", "value": "{{ReorderLevel}}" }
                      ]
                    }
                  ]
                }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
            }
          }

Handlebars is more advanced than SpEL, but it unlocks more powerful data manipulation. Most notably, it provides the ability to dynamically generate list elements from an array. The goal here is to take the products array returned by the OData API and turn each product into a list element, with its name as the title and its price as a detail attribute. Without Handlebars, you would need a fixed, hardcoded structure; with it, you can loop over however many products the API returns and build the elements array at runtime.

When scripting_type: handlebars is set, the entire message: block becomes a folded JSON string. The message type, content fields, and the elements array are all rendered by the Handlebars engine before Joule parses the result. {{#eachJoin products}} iterates the array and dynamically creates one list element per product. Use {{#eachJoin}} instead of the standard {{#each}} when building JSON arrays. It automatically handles comma separation between items, so you don't need the {{#unless @Last}},{{/unless}} workaround. Setting enableDetailView: true on the content makes each list item clickable, opening a detail panel on the right populated from the sections array on that element. Each section has a title and an attributes list of label/value pairs.

Message Type: List

When to use: search results, catalog browsing, or any multi-record response where the user needs to compare or scan across items.

Carousel Messages

The carousel type renders a horizontally scrollable row of cards. Unlike list, which stacks items vertically and supports expanded detail sections, carousel is optimized for quick visual comparison of a small set of entities. Each card has a title, subtitle, image, optional status, and buttons.

The sample capability fetches Northwind product categories and renders one card per category. Each card includes a postback button that, when clicked, sends a new utterance back to Joule to trigger the list capability filtered by that category's ID. This creates a two-step flow where you browse categories in the carousel, then drill into products for a selected category in a list.

# northwind_category_carousel/functions/get_categories_carousel.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: api-request
        method: GET
        system_alias: NorthwindService
        path: "/Categories?$select=CategoryID,CategoryName,Description"
        result_variable: api_result
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: categories
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: message
        scripting_type: handlebars
        message: >
          {
            "type": "carousel",
            "delay": 0,
            "content": {
              "enableDetailView": false,
              "cards": [
                {{#eachJoin categories}}
                  {
                    "title": "{{CategoryName}}",
                    "subtitle": "{{Description}}",
                    "imageUrl": "sap-icon://product",
                    "buttons": [
                      {
                        "type": "postback",
                        "title": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "text": "Explore {{CategoryName}}",
                        "value": "List products in category {{CategoryID}}"
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
            }
          }

The postback button's value field, "List products in category {{CategoryID}}", is the utterance that Joule receives when the user clicks "Explore Beverages". The list capability's scenario description includes "list products in a specific category" and declares category_id as a slot, so Joule picks it up and extracts the numeric ID from that utterance. The list function then applies $filter=CategoryID%20eq%20<? category_id ?> to the OData request.

Message Type: Carousel

When to use: a small, fixed set of entities where side-by-side comparison is useful and each item has a natural follow-on action, such as drilling into a filtered view.

Media Messages

The media type embeds an image or video directly in the chat window. For video, Joule supports YouTube, Vimeo, Daily Motion, SAP Video, and SAP TV Video URLs. For direct file links, supported formats are MP4, WEBM, OGV, and 3GP. All URLs must be publicly accessible.

The sample capability for this section is intentionally simple, with no API call, no parameters, and no slots. It demonstrates that a capability does not need to reach a backend service to be useful. The function sends a short text message followed by the video embed in a single unconditional action group.

# sapphire_2026_media/functions/get_video.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Here's the SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote."
      - type: message
        message:
          type: media
          content: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CocpyxAizwE"

The two message actions in the same action group send back-to-back. The text message appears first, giving the embed context before the video player renders below it. This chaining pattern applies to any message type combination: you can follow a card with a buttons message, or a text summary with a list of results, by placing multiple message actions in the same action group.

The content field takes the URL directly as a string without additional nesting required. For a YouTube URL, Joule renders an embedded player. The user can play the video without leaving the chat.

Message Type: Media

When to use: product demo videos, how-to recordings, keynote highlights, or any response where a visual or audio asset communicates more than text alone.

Buttons Messages

The buttons type renders one or more clickable action buttons, optionally preceded by a summary text. It is the right choice when you want to give the user a clear set of next steps directly in the response, or when a single result can be explored in multiple ways.

This capability is the launcher for the rest of the post. Ask Joule to "show me different Joule message types in action" and it renders one postback button per message type. Clicking a button sends the right prompt back to Joule and triggers that capability without the user having to type anything.

# northwind_product_buttons/functions/show_message_type_launcher.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: buttons
          content:
            title: "Pick a message type to see it in action:"
            buttons:
              - type: postback
                title: See as text
                value: "Show me product Chai as text"
              - type: postback
                title: See as card
                value: "Show me product Chai as a card"
              - type: postback
                title: Browse all products via list
                value: "Provide me a list of products"
              - type: postback
                title: See categories as carousel
                value: "Show me Northwind categories"
              - type: postback
                title: See products as integration card
                value: "Show me products as an integration card"
              - type: postback
                title: See as illustrated message
                value: "Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
              - type: postback
                title: See as media (SAPPHIRE video)
                value: "What happened at SAPPHIRE 2026"
              - type: postback
                title: Rate Joule (Likert)
                value: "Rate my Joule experience"

The function has no parameters, no slots, and no API call. Chai is hardcoded in each postback value that requires a product name, so every downstream capability receives a valid input when the button is clicked.

Some button types available in Joule:

  • postback: sends the value string back to Joule as a new user message, continuing the conversation
  • navigation: opens a URL in the browser or navigates within the host application
  • clientdata: passes a silent payload to the host application without sending a visible message

Message Type: Buttons

When to use: after delivering a result when the logical next action is predictable, or when a single entity can be viewed in multiple ways and you want to let the user choose without typing follow-up prompts.

Likert Scale Messages

The likert_scale type renders a row of labeled, clickable scale options directly in the chat. It is designed for in-chat surveys and feedback collection where you need a numeric rating on a defined scale. When the user clicks an option, the selected value is stored in a slot and becomes available to downstream steps in the same dialog function.

The supported range is 1 to 7 options. The compiler does not enforce contiguous values or prevent duplicates, which gives you flexibility to configure non-standard scales. In practice a simple 1-to-5 or 1-to-7 Likert scale is the most common use case.

The sample capability asks the user to rate how well Joule fits their needs, stores the response in a slot, and sends a personalised thank-you text message.

The scenario declares the slot so Joule knows to wait for the Likert response before advancing:

# northwind_product_likert/scenarios/joule_satisfaction.yaml
description: >-
  Capture the user's satisfaction with Joule. Use this when the user
  wants to rate their experience or answer a survey question about Joule.

slots:
  - name: joule_fit_for_purpose
    description: >-
      The user's rating of whether Joule does what they need, on a scale
      from 1 (Strongly disagree) to 5 (Strongly agree).

target:
  type: function
  name: scenario_joule_survey

The function sends the scale first, then branches on the captured slot value:

# northwind_product_likert/functions/scenario_joule_survey.yaml (relevant section)
action_groups:
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose == null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: likert_scale
          content:
            prompt: "Joule does what I need it to do"
            options:
              - label: "Strongly disagree"
                value: 1
              - label: "Disagree"
                value: 2
              - label: "Neutral"
                value: 3
              - label: "Agree"
                value: 4
              - label: "Strongly agree"
                value: 5
  - condition: joule_fit_for_purpose != null
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "Thank you! You rated Joule's fit for purpose as <? joule_fit_for_purpose ?> out of 5."

The first action group fires when the slot is empty, displaying the scale. Once the user clicks an option, the slot is populated and Joule re-enters the function, this time matching the second action group and sending the confirmation text.

A few practical notes:

  • The likert_scale message type is designed to work with slot filling. Declare the slot in the scenario so Joule waits for the user's selection before proceeding.
  • The prompt field is the question text displayed above the scale. Keep it short, one sentence.
  • options requires at least one entry and at most seven. Values do not need to be contiguous.
  • The selected value is stored as a number in the slot, so downstream SpEL comparisons use numeric operators, such as joule_fit_for_purpose >= 4.

Message Type: Likert Scale

When to use: in-chat feedback forms, satisfaction surveys, or any scenario where you need a structured numeric rating from the user without leaving the chat.

UI Integration Cards

How it differs from a plain card message

The card message type is a fixed Joule UI component with a predefined layout. It has a title, subtitle, image, status, and description. You populate fields but you cannot change the layout itself.

The ui5integrationCard type embeds a full UI5 Integration Card driven by a JSON manifest. You control the card type, the column layout, field grouping, labels, and semantic coloring. The trade-off is verbosity: the manifest is considerably longer than a card message, and all data must come from the dialog function. The card manifest cannot reach out to a BTP destination directly.

Card types available

The UI5 Integration Card framework supports a range of card types, each suited to different data shapes and presentation needs:

Card typeBest for
ObjectSingle-record detail view with grouped label/value fields
TableMulti-record tabular display with columns, alignment, and state badges
ListCompact vertically stacked items with icon, title, description
AnalyticalCharts: bar, line, donut, bullet, stacked bar
TimelineChronological event sequences
CalendarDate-based scheduling views
ComponentCustom UI5 component embed (not supported in Joule)

The full catalog with live examples is in the UI5 Integration Card Explorer.

The Table card

The Table card is well suited for multi-record results. It renders columns with headers, supports right-alignment for numeric values, and displays semantic state badges with icons on any column using state, showStateIcon, and inverted.

The sample capability fetches up to 10 products with their category name by extending the Category entity, as well as the stock status, and renders them as a table with four columns: Product, Category, In Stock, and Status.

The key pattern is a two-step construction:

  1. Build the rows array with Handlebars, computing status and statusState per row inline using {{#if}} comparisons against UnitsInStock and Discontinued
  2. Inject the rows into the manifest via SpEL and send it as a message action
# northwind_product_integration_card/functions/get_product_integration_card.yaml
# ... (parameters and api-request action groups follow the same pattern as earlier examples)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: products
            value: <? api_result.body.value ?>
      - type: set-variables
        scripting_type: handlebars
        variables:
          - name: tableRows
            value: >
              [
                {{#eachJoin products}}
                {
                  "product": "{{ProductName}}",
                  "category": "{{Category.CategoryName}}",
                  "unitsInStock": "{{UnitsInStock}}",
                  "status": "{{#if Discontinued}}Discontinued{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Out of Stock{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Low Stock{{else}}In Stock{{/if}}",
                  "statusState": "{{#if Discontinued}}Error{{else if (eq UnitsInStock 0)}}Error{{else if (lt UnitsInStock 10)}}Warning{{else}}Success{{/if}}"
                }
                {{/eachJoin}}
              ]
      - type: message
        scripting_type: spel
        message: >
          {
            "type": "ui5integrationCard",
            "content": {
              "_version": "1.17.0",
              "sap.app": { "type": "card", "id": "northwind.product.tablecard" },
              "sap.card": {
                "type": "Table",
                "header": {
                  "title": "Northwind Products",
                  "subtitle": "Showing up to 10 products"
                },
                "content": {
                  "data": { "json": <? tableRows ?> },
                  "maxItems": 10,
                  "row": {
                    "columns": [
                      { "title": "Product",   "value": "{product}",      "identifier": true },
                      { "title": "Category",  "value": "{category}" },
                      { "title": "In Stock",  "value": "{unitsInStock}", "hAlign": "End" },
                      {
                        "title": "Status",
                        "value": "{status}",
                        "state": "{statusState}",
                        "showStateIcon": true,
                        "inverted": true
                      }
                    ]
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }

A few points worth noting:

  • $expand=Category fetches the related Category entity in a single OData call.
  • data.json placement: in Table cards, data sits inside content, not at the sap.card level. The row.columns then use {product}, {category} etc. as binding paths into the JSON array.
  • State badges: "state": "{statusState}" with "showStateIcon": true and "inverted": true produces the filled colored badge with icon seen in the UI5 Card Explorer examples. Valid state values are Success, Warning, Error, Information, and None.
  • Two-step construction: Handlebars builds tableRows as a JSON array string, then SpEL injects it into the manifest via <? tableRows ?>. This is necessary because the YAML schema validator rejects SpEL expressions in positions that expect a literal array.

Message Type: UI Integration Card

When to use: multi-record results where column layout, numeric alignment, or semantic state coloring adds clarity that a plain list message cannot provide. For a single-record detail view, the Object card type is a better fit. The full range of UI5 card types, Analytical charts, Timeline, Calendar, opens up significant display possibilities beyond what Joule's built-in message types support.

Key restrictions for ui5integrationCard in Joule:

  • Only static data bindings are supported. You cannot declare a BTP destination inside the manifest and have Joule fetch data from it at render time.
  • Action handlers defined in the manifest are not executed by Joule.
  • Component card types are not supported.
  • Filters and user-defined manifest parameters are not supported.

Illustrated Messages

The illustrated_message type renders an SAP illustration alongside a title, subtitle, and up to two call-to-action buttons. It is designed for the moments that bookend a transaction or search, such as a success confirmation, a not-found state, or a system error. Rather than showing a blank text response after an action, it gives the user a visual signal that something meaningful happened.

The sample capability looks up a Northwind product and branches into two states based on what the API returns:

  • Product found, in stock, and not discontinued: Success illustration
  • Product found but discontinued or out of stock: UnableToLoad illustration
  • Product not found at all: tntEmpty illustration
# northwind_product_illustrated/functions/get_product_illustrated.yaml (relevant section)
  - condition: api_result.status_code == 200 && api_result.body.value != null && api_result.body.value.size() > 0
    actions:
      - type: set-variables
        variables:
          - name: product
            value: <? api_result.body.value[0] ?>
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: "<? (product.Discontinued == true || product.UnitsInStock == 0) ? 'UnableToLoad' : 'TntSuccess' ?>"
            title: "<? product.ProductName ?>"
            subtitle: "$<? product.UnitPrice ?> · <? product.UnitsInStock ?> units in stock"
            buttons:
              - title: Look up another product
                type: postback
                text: Look up another product
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"
              - title: Browse catalog
                type: navigation
                navigation_target:
                  url: "https://services.odata.org/V3/Northwind/Northwind.svc/Products"
  - condition: api_result.status_code != 200 || api_result.body.value == null || api_result.body.value.size() == 0
    actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: illustrated_message
          content:
            illustration_name: NoEntries
            title: "Product not found"
            subtitle: "No product matching '<? product_name ?>' was found in the Northwind catalog."
            buttons:
              - title: Try again
                type: postback
                text: Try again
                value: "Show me another product as an illustrated message"

The illustration_name is a SpEL expression that evaluates to UnableToLoad when the product is discontinued or out of stock, and TntSuccess otherwise. The not-found branch uses the separate NoEntries illustrated message.

The buttons array supports the same button types as the card and buttons message types: postback, navigation, phonenumber, and clientdata. The postback button's value field is the utterance Joule receives when the user clicks. Note it includes "as an illustrated message" to keep Joule routing to this capability rather than a different one for the next lookup.

A few practical notes:

  • For inspiration on illustration names, browse the UI5 Web Components IllustratedMessageType. To see how they look, check the UI5 IllustratedMessage samples.
  • The maximum number of buttons is two.

Message Type: Illustrated Message

When to use: after a mutation (create, update, delete) to confirm the outcome, or when a search returns no results and you want to guide the user toward a recovery action rather than leaving them with a silent empty state.

Common Pitfalls

A few mistakes that are easy to make when building capabilities for the first time:

  • URL-encode dynamic values in OData paths. Spaces in a user-supplied slot value (e.g. "Chai Tea") will break an OData filter if embedded directly. Add a set-variables step first with <? product_name.urlEncode() ?>, then reference the encoded variable in the path.
  • Use {{#eachJoin}} instead of {{#each}} when building JSON arrays in Handlebars. {{#eachJoin}} inserts commas between items automatically. {{#each}} does not, which produces invalid JSON.
  • In Table integration cards, data belongs inside content, not at the sap.card level. Placing it at the wrong level causes the card to render empty.
  • likert_scale stores the selected value as a number. Downstream SpEL comparisons must use numeric operators (>=, ==), not string comparisons.
  • Postback button value strings must match the target capability's scenario description. Joule uses the value as an utterance for intent matching. Include the keywords that route to the right capability.

UI Constraints and Limits

Each message type enforces hard limits on element counts and text lengths. Exceeding these silently truncates or hides content. The full tables covering element counts, character limits, full-screen widths, and markdown variants are in the UI Constraints and Limits page of the Joule Development Guide.

Try It Yourself

The complete sample files are in this GitHub folder. The assistant is structured as one Digital Assistant file referencing nine separate capability folders, one per message type. Each capability's scenario description includes a phrase like "as text", "as a card", or "as a list". Joule then uses these during intent matching to route the incoming prompt to the correct capability. Once deployed, ask for any Northwind product with buttons to get a clickable launcher that triggers each message type without typing the prompts manually.

Deploy everything in one step:

cd Blog_01_joule_message_types
joule deploy -c -n "northwind_message_types"
joule launch "northwind_message_types"

Try each of these prompts to exercise all nine message types:

Message typeSample prompt
Text"Show me product Chai as text"
Card"Show me product Chai as a card"
Buttons"Show me different Joule message types in action"
List"Provide me a list of products"
Carousel"Show me Northwind categories"
UI Integration Card"Show me products as an integration card"
Illustrated Message"Show me product Chai as an illustrated message"
Likert Scale"Rate your Joule experience"
Media"Show me the SAPPHIRE 2026 keynote"

For the carousel, click the "Explore" button on any category card to trigger the list capability filtered by that category. The list capability also works standalone. "List products in category 1" will return only Beverages products directly.

For the illustrated message, try a product that exists ("Show me product Chai as an illustrated message") to see the success state, and a name that matches nothing ("Show me product XYZ as an illustrated message") to see the not-found empty state.

What is Next

In the next post we look at restricting which scenarios are visible to which users using IAS group membership and BTP role assignments. See Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Role-Based Scenario Access in Joule. More posts in this series are on the way.

Resources

  • Messages — Joule Development Guide
  • UI Integration Cards — Joule Development Guide
  • UI5 Integration Card Explorer
